summary: A Chinese dynasty that ruled from 206 BCE to 220 CE, succeeding the Qin dynasty and giving its name to China's majority ethnic group (the Han Chinese). Founded by the peasant rebel Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu), who defeated his rival Xiang Yu at the Battle of Gaixia after the Chu-Han Contention. Split into a Western/Former Han and an Eastern/Later Han by the brief Xin Dynasty interregnum of the usurper Wang Mang, it was ruled for a long stretch by the expansionist Emperor Wu (Wudi), fought the nomadic Xiongnu while pioneering the heqin marriage-alliance policy, conquered Nanyue and other southern Yue kingdoms (briefly losing Vietnam to the Trung Sisters' Rebellion), and produced the historian Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian. Its final decades were consumed by the Daoist-inspired Yellow Turban Rebellion and warlord infighting, leading to its collapse and the Three Kingdoms period.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the dynasty is identified in giveaways: a Chinese dynasty founded by the peasant rebel Liu Bang after he overthrew the Qin dynasty, ruling from about 206 BCE to 220 CE and giving its name to China's majority Han ethnic group. Its rule is conventionally split into a Western (or Former) Han and an Eastern (or Later) Han period, separated by an interregnum, and its height was roughly contemporaneous with the Roman Empire.

clues.clue: Founded by the peasant rebel Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu), who overthrew the Qin dynasty
examples: name this Chinese dynasty founded by Liu Bang, whose collapse led to the Three Kingdoms Period.
Liu Bang rose from the peasantry to found this dynasty which succeeded the Qin.

clues.clue: Gives its name to China's majority ethnic group, the Han Chinese
examples: the Trung sisters rebelled against what Chinese dynasty, which lends its name to China's majority ethnic group?
Wu Di was a prominent emperor of this dynasty... It names China's dominant ethnic group.

clues.clue: Ruled roughly from 206 BCE to 220 CE, conventionally divided into a Western/Former Han and an Eastern/Later Han
examples: name this Chinese dynasty founded by Liu Bang that was in power from 206 BC to 220 CE.
Wang Mang interrupted the rule of this dynasty, which is thus divided into Western and Eastern periods.

clues.clue: Its height was roughly contemporaneous with the Roman Empire (the Pax Romana and Emperor Augustus)
examples: the Pax Romana was roughly contemporary with the 'Eastern' period of what dynasty that succeeded the Qin?
Wu Di was a prominent emperor of this dynasty, which controlled the eastern part of the Silk Road as Augustus declared himself emperor of Rome.

works.name: Founding: Liu Bang and the Chu-Han Contention
works.description: The dynasty was founded by Liu Bang, a commoner from Chu's Pei County who rebelled against the Qin alongside the warlord Xiang Yu before the two turned on each other in the Chu-Han Contention. After escaping an assassination attempt at the Feast at Hong Gate, Liu Bang defeated Xiang Yu at the decisive Battle of Gaixia, ending the rival Eighteen Kingdoms and founding the new dynasty.

clues.clue: Liu Bang defeated his rival Xiang Yu at the Battle of Gaixia, ending the Chu-Han Contention and founding the dynasty
examples: The warlord Xiang Yu was defeated at the Battle of Gaixia by this dynasty's founder Liu Bang.
The founder of this dynasty won the Battle of Gaixia after escaping a dangerous dinner with a rival in the Feast at Hong Gate.

clues.clue: Before their war, Xiang Yu tried to assassinate Liu Bang at a banquet known as the Feast at Hong Gate
examples: The founder of this dynasty won the Battle of Gaixia after escaping a dangerous dinner with a rival in the Feast at (*) Hong Gate.

clues.clue: The establishment of the rival Eighteen Kingdoms began the dynasty's founding contention with Xiang Yu's state of Western Chu
examples: The establishment of the Eighteen Kingdoms began this dynasty's contention with the Western Chu.
Its founder came to power after overcoming Xiang Yu's state of Chu.

clues.clue: Legend holds that a drunk Liu Bang killed the 'White Emperor' after cutting off the head of a white snake
examples: A legend about the founder of this dynasty states that while drunk, he killed the White Emperor after cutting off the head of a white snake.

clues.clue: Huai II made Liu Bang the King of Guanzhong after he captured the city of Xianyang
examples: Huai II made this dynasty's founder the King of Guanzhong after he captured the city of Xianyang.

works.name: Empress Lu and the Early Succession Crisis
works.description: After founder Liu Bang's death from a campaign wound, his wife Empress Lu (Lu Zhi) became the first Empress of China and ruled as a ruthless regent, purging rivals to secure power for her own family before the dynasty's throne reverted to the Liu line.

clues.clue: Empress Lu became the first Empress of China after Liu Bang's death, killing the general Peng Yue and being accused of murdering the three princes of Zhao
examples: identify this dynasty whose Empress Lu became the first Empress of China after marrying Liu Bang.
This dynasty's first empress had the general Peng Yue killed and was accused of murdering the three princes of Zhao.

clues.clue: Empress Dowager Lu tortured Liu Bang's concubine Qi into a 'human swine' by cutting off her limbs and gouging out her eyes
examples: To turn her into 'human swine', Concubine Qi's limbs were cut off and her eyes gouged out by this dynasty's Empress Dowager Lu.

clues.clue: Liu Bang died from an injury sustained while campaigning against the rebel Ying Bu, declaring that even the legendary physician Bian Que could not save him
examples: The founder of this dynasty declared even Bian Que could not save him before his death from an injury sustained on campaign against Ying Bu.

works.name: Emperor Wu (Wudi) and Sima Qian
works.description: Emperor Wu (Wudi) presided over the dynasty's long imperial expansion, sending expeditions for the fabled 'heavenly horses' and against the Xiongnu while establishing the National University (Taixue) and making Confucianism the state doctrine after banning the Hundred Schools of Thought. His reign is also chronicled by the court historian Sima Qian, whom Wudi had castrated rather than executed for defending a general who defected to the Xiongnu, and who went on to write the dynasty's early history in the Records of the Grand Historian.

clues.clue: Sima Qian wrote the Records of the Grand Historian, which chronicles much of the dynasty's early history
examples: Much of this dynasty's early history is recorded in a history book written during this dynasty, The Records of the Grand Historian, written by Sima Qian.
Sima Qian wrote the Records of the Grand Historian during the reign of Wudi, an emperor of this dynasty.

clues.clue: Sima Qian was castrated rather than executed as punishment for defending the general Li Ling, who had defected to the Xiongnu
examples: One ruler from this line sent an expedition in search of the heavenly horses, had the writer of the Records of the Grand Historian castrated, and instituted monopolies on salt and iron.
Another expedition under this dynasty ended in disaster and the capture of Gen. (*) Li Ling.

clues.clue: A ruler established the 'National University' (Taixue) and made Confucianism the state doctrine after banning the Hundred Schools of Thought
examples: A ruler of this dynasty established the 'National University' after banning the Hundred Schools of Thought, and this dynasty began after a war with the Chu state.
This dynasty's official Sang Hongyang instituted state control of grain and liquor under an emperor who established Gojoseon's 'Four Commanderies' and Taixue's Imperial Academy.

works.name: Salt and Iron Monopolies
works.description: Emperor Wu instituted state monopolies on salt and iron production to fund his wars and southward conquests. The merchant-turned-official Sang Hongyang extended this fiscal program with an 'equable marketing system' for grain and the state-sponsored 'Discourses on Salt and Iron' debates; the monopolies were never revived after Emperor Zhang's death.

clues.clue: Instituted state monopolies on salt and iron production; official Sang Hongyang developed the 'equable marketing system' for grain and the 'Discourses on Salt and Iron'
examples: During this dynasty, the former merchant Sang Hongyang developed a form of grain supply management called the 'equable marketing system' and facilitated the Discourses on Salt and Iron.
It began the practice of monopolizing salt and iron production and frequently warred with the nomadic Xiongnu.

clues.clue: The salt and iron monopolies funded the dynasty's southward conquest of the Yue
examples: Monopolies on salt and iron funded this dynasty's southward conquest of the Yue, which began the first 'Northern Domination' under the Legalist rule of Wudi.

clues.clue: The ruinous salt and iron monopolies were never revived after Emperor Zhang's death, per the histories of Fan Ye and Ban Gu covering the dynasty's 'Former' and 'Later' periods
examples: The histories of Fan Zhe and Ban Gu chronicle the 'Former' and 'Later' periods of this dynasty which, after Emperor Zhang's death, never revived its ruinous monopolies on salt and iron.

works.name: Xiongnu Wars and the Heqin Marriage-Alliance Policy
works.description: The dynasty fought recurring wars against the nomadic Xiongnu confederation to its north. After an early defeat at the Battle of Baideng, it pioneered heqin, marrying tribute princesses like Wang Zhaojun to Xiongnu chanyu such as Huhanye to buy peace, while later generals like Ban Chao led expeditions deep into Central Asia's Western Regions.

clues.clue: After a defeat at the Battle of Baideng, the dynasty married tribute princesses to Xiongnu leaders (chanyu) under the heqin policy, including Wang Zhaojun's marriage to Huhanye
examples: This dynasty's defeat at the Battle of Baideng led to the marriage of one of the 'Four Beauties,' Wang Zhaojun, to Huhanye.
The first known marriage alliances called heqin occurred during this dynasty's rule, leading to ten of their princesses being married off to foreign holders of the title chanyu.

clues.clue: General Ban Chao led an expedition into the Western Regions that captured Kara Shahr and Kucha, while another expedition ended in disaster
examples: A military expedition during this dynasty captured the cities of Kara Shahr and Kucha, and was led by General Ban Chao.
One army under this dynasty fought naked in order to shame an opposing army that contained several regiments of women.

clues.clue: Diplomat Lady Feng Liao averted a conflict; Princess Liu Xijun, sent off under heqin, wrote a melancholic poem calling her new home 'the edge of the world'
examples: A diplomat from this dynasty, Lady Feng Liao, avoided a conflict in a place that Princess Liu Xijun called 'the edge of the world' in a melancholic poem.

works.name: Ban Zhao and Women's Education
works.description: The court historian Ban Zhao, sister of the historian Ban Gu, finished her brother's history after his death and imprisonment and wrote the standard women's-education text Lessons for Women, prescribing submissive feminine virtues alongside other Han-era instructional texts on women's conduct.

clues.clue: Female historian Ban Zhao wrote Lessons for Women, detailing four ideal, submissive feminine virtues, after finishing the history left unfinished by her imprisoned and deceased brother Ban Gu
examples: Four ideal feminine virtues were detailed in Lessons for Women by a historian from this dynasty, Ban Zhao.
A female historian of this dynasty advised women to be submissive to their husbands in Lessons for Women.

clues.clue: A law during this dynasty quintupled the poll tax for families with unmarried daughters between the ages of 15 and 30
examples: A law introduced during this dynasty quintupled the poll tax for families with unmarried daughters between the ages of 15 and 30.

works.name: Southward Expansion: Nanyue and the Trung Sisters' Rebellion
works.description: Under Emperor Wu, the dynasty conquered the southern Nanyue and Dian kingdoms as part of its subjugation of the 'Hundred Yue' peoples, including the Trieu dynasty and the Minyue and Giao Chi peoples, beginning what is remembered as the first 'Northern Domination' of Vietnam. That rule was briefly interrupted by the Trung Sisters' Rebellion, in which the sisters were remembered riding into battle on war elephants.

clues.clue: Under Emperor Wu, the dynasty conquered the Nanyue and Dian kingdoms as part of its subjugation of the 'Hundred Yue,' including the Trieu dynasty
examples: As part of its conquest of the Hundred Yue, this dynasty conquered the Trieu dynasty.
This dynasty's southward expansion included the conquest of Nanyue and Dian kingdoms under Emperor Wu.

clues.clue: The Trung Sisters led a rebellion against Han rule in Vietnam, remembered riding into battle on war elephants
examples: This dynasty fought a pair of women often depicted riding war elephants during its invasion of Vietnam.
One source for the Trung sisters' rebellion is a history by Fan Ye chronicling the 'Later' period of this dynasty.

clues.clue: The dynasty campaigned against Minyue in retaliation for an invasion of the Eastern Ou, and dominated the Giao Chi people
examples: In retaliation for an invasion of the Eastern Ou, this dynasty launched a campaign against the state of Minyue.
This dynasty's domination of the Giao Chi people was briefly threatened by a peasant revolt led by Tay Vu Vuong.

clues.clue: Merchants trading with 'southwest barbarians' reported a Sendhuk community, believed by modern scholars to be a lost Burmese state or an Indus Valley settlement
examples: During trading expeditions to 'southwest barbarians,' merchants from this dynasty reported the existence of a Sendhuk community believed by modern scholars to be either a lost Burmese state or an Indus Valley settlement.

works.name: Wang Mang's Xin Dynasty Interregnum
works.description: The Confucian scholar-official Wang Mang seized power amid a succession crisis (following the death of the 'Lamentable Emperor' Ai and the child Emperor Ping), deposing the dynasty and founding his own short-lived Xin Dynasty, during which he attempted to revive the ancient well-field land system. The peasant 'Red Eyebrows' rebellion eventually overthrew him and restored Han rule in its 'Eastern' form.

clues.clue: The Confucian scholar-official Wang Mang deposed the child Emperor Ping (after the crisis caused by the death of the homosexual 'Lamentable Emperor' Ai) and founded his own Xin Dynasty, interrupting Han rule
examples: One member of this dynasty was a homosexual posthumously titled the 'Lamentable emperor'. His death, followed by the death of the infant Emperor Ping, triggered a dynastic crisis during this dynasty.
This dynasty was interrupted by the Xin Dynasty of Wang Mang.

clues.clue: Peasants painted their eyebrows red to rebel as the 'Red Eyebrows,' eventually overthrowing Wang Mang and restoring Han rule
examples: Peasants painted their eyebrows red to stand against a court official who rebelled and interrupted this dynasty with the Xin.
Domestically, this dynasty was restored following the Red Eyebrows' deposition of Wang Mang.

clues.clue: Wang Mang attempted to revive the ancient well-field land distribution system during his usurpation
examples: Wang Mang, who usurped this Chinese dynasty, attempted to revive the Well-Field System.
Wang Mang temporarily restored the well-field system while usurping this Chinese dynasty.

works.name: Administration and Science
works.description: The dynasty organized its territory into commanderies and, later, higher-level zhou provinces, collected tribute in kind (paying officials in dan of grain), and hosted the polymath scientist-poet Zhang Heng, who worked at the second capital Luoyang.

clues.clue: Created the zhou, a higher administrative subdivision above the roughly 160 commanderies it controlled
examples: This dynasty created a type of province called a zhou as a higher administrative subdivision than the 160 commanderies it controlled.

clues.clue: Polymath Zhang Heng invented a hydraulic armillary sphere and a seismometer while working at the dynasty's second capital, Luoyang
examples: The polymath Zhang Heng invented a hydraulic armillary sphere and seismometer in this dynasty's second capital of Luoyang.

clues.clue: Zhang Heng also wrote a rhapsodic 'fu'-style poem about the springs of Mount Li
examples: Zhang Heng's poem written during this dynasty about the springs of Mount Li is an example of a rhapsodic style of poetry popular during it called fu.

clues.clue: Collected tribute in kind, paying officials in dan of grain, with the highest salary going to the Three Ducal Ministers
examples: Since this state collected tribute in kind, it paid salaries to its officials in dan of grain, the highest salary being received by the Three Ducal Ministers.

clues.clue: In 1968, explosives unearthed the mausoleum of Liu Sheng of Zhongshan, where molten metal had sealed off Dou Wan's jade burial suit
examples: In 1968, military explosives unearthed this dynasty's mausoleum for Liu Sheng of Zhongshan, where molten metal sealed off Dou Wan's jade burial suit.

works.name: Decline and Fall: the Yellow Turban Rebellion
works.description: In its final decades, Daoist movements preaching the 'Way of Supreme Peace' sparked the Yellow Turban Rebellion under the Zhang brothers, while the related Celestial Masters sect led the 'Five Pecks of Rice' rebellion. A succession struggle involving Empress He, her brother He Jin, and court eunuchs let the warlord Dong Zhuo seize power, and the dynasty formally ended when Emperor Xian abdicated to Cao Pi, giving way to the Three Kingdoms period.

clues.clue: The Daoist 'Way of Supreme Peace' inspired the Yellow Turban Rebellion, led by the Zhang brothers, which greatly weakened the dynasty and led to the Three Kingdoms period
examples: A rebellion against this dynasty preached the Way of Supreme Peace and was led by three Zhang brothers.
The Yellow Turban Rebellion greatly weakened this dynasty, which soon gave way to the Three Kingdoms period.

clues.clue: The related Celestial Masters Daoist sect led the 'Five Pecks of Rice' rebellion late in the dynasty
examples: A group called the Celestial Masters emerged late in this dynasty and led the Five Pecks of Rice rebellion.
Towards its end, this dynasty faced rebellions led by Daoists like the Way of the Supreme Peace and Five Pecks of Rice.

clues.clue: A succession struggle between Empress He, her brother He Jin, and a faction of court eunuchs let the warlord Dong Zhuo seize power
examples: A succession struggle during this dynasty between Empress He, her brother He Jin, and a faction of eunuchs allowed Dong Zhuo to come to power.
Dong Zhuo's tyranny led to the decline of this dynasty and the advent of the Three Kingdoms, when it was controlled by the state of Cao Wei.

clues.clue: The dynasty formally ended when Emperor Xian abdicated to Cao Pi, son of the warlord Cao Cao who had contended with Sun Quan and Liu Bei
examples: This dynasty ended with the abdication of Emperor Xian to Cao Pi, whose father was a warlord who contended with Sun Quan and Liu Bei.

clues.clue: An emperor coined the phrase 'thorn in my back' to describe general Huo Guang, who deposed Prince He after twenty-eight days
examples: The phrase 'thorn in my back' was coined by an emperor of this dynasty to describe his general Huo Guang, who deposed Prince He after twenty eight days.

clues.clue: Intrigue between scholar-officials and student 'partisans' (dangren) prompted a civil-liberties crackdown later lifted by Emperor Ling, after an earlier emperor had engineer Bi Lan build a chain-pump water system to placate unrest
examples: Intrigue during this dynasty involving scholars and the students who supported them, called dangren or 'partisans,' prompted a civil liberties crackdown that was lifted by Emperor Ling.
An emperor of this dynasty tried to dissuade discontent by having the engineer Bi Lan construct a chain-pump water delivery system in the capital.

comprehensive_summary: The Han Dynasty ruled China from roughly 206 BCE to 220 CE, succeeding the Qin dynasty and eventually giving way to the Three Kingdoms period; it also gave its name to China's majority Han ethnic group and was roughly contemporaneous with the Roman Empire. It was founded by the peasant rebel Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu), who rebelled against the Qin alongside the warlord Xiang Yu before the two turned on each other in the Chu-Han Contention; after escaping an assassination attempt at the Feast at Hong Gate and overcoming the rival Eighteen Kingdoms, Liu Bang defeated Xiang Yu at the decisive Battle of Gaixia. Legend holds that a drunk Liu Bang had earlier killed the 'White Emperor' after beheading a white snake, and Huai II had made him King of Guanzhong after he captured Xianyang. After Liu Bang's death from a wound sustained fighting the rebel Ying Bu, his wife Empress Lu became the first Empress of China, ruthlessly purging rivals — killing the general Peng Yue and torturing concubine Qi into a 'human swine.' The dynasty's long imperial height came under Emperor Wu (Wudi), who established the National University (Taixue) and made Confucianism the state doctrine after banning the Hundred Schools of Thought, instituted state monopolies on salt and iron (extended by official Sang Hongyang's 'equable marketing system' and the 'Discourses on Salt and Iron'), and had the court historian Sima Qian castrated rather than executed for defending a general who defected to the Xiongnu; Sima Qian went on to write the Records of the Grand Historian, chronicling much of the dynasty's early history. The dynasty fought recurring wars against the nomadic Xiongnu, and after an early defeat at the Battle of Baideng pioneered the heqin policy of marrying tribute princesses like Wang Zhaojun to Xiongnu chanyu such as Huhanye to buy peace; General Ban Chao later led expeditions into Central Asia's Western Regions, capturing Kara Shahr and Kucha, while diplomat Lady Feng Liao averted conflict and princess-poet Liu Xijun lamented being sent to 'the edge of the world.' The historian Ban Zhao, sister of Ban Gu, finished her brother's unfinished history and wrote the standard women's-education text Lessons for Women. Under Emperor Wu the dynasty also conquered the southern Nanyue and Dian kingdoms as part of a wider subjugation of the 'Hundred Yue' (including the Trieu dynasty, Minyue, and the Giao Chi people), beginning the 'Northern Domination' of Vietnam, though this rule was briefly interrupted by the Trung Sisters' Rebellion. Domestically, the Confucian scholar-official Wang Mang deposed the child Emperor Ping amid a succession crisis and founded his own short-lived Xin Dynasty, attempting to revive the ancient well-field land system, before the peasant 'Red Eyebrows' rebellion overthrew him and restored Han rule in its 'Eastern' form — the conventional dividing line between the Western/Former Han and Eastern/Later Han. The dynasty organized its territory into commanderies and, later, zhou provinces, collected tribute in kind, and was home to the polymath Zhang Heng, who invented a hydraulic armillary sphere and seismometer at the second capital Luoyang. In its final decades, the Daoist 'Way of Supreme Peace' inspired the Yellow Turban Rebellion under the Zhang brothers (alongside the Celestial Masters' related 'Five Pecks of Rice' rebellion), while a succession struggle between Empress He, her brother He Jin, and court eunuchs let the warlord Dong Zhuo seize power. The dynasty formally ended when Emperor Xian abdicated to Cao Pi, son of the warlord Cao Cao who had contended with Sun Quan and Liu Bei, giving way to the Three Kingdoms period.
recursive_suggestions: Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu) — the dynasty's founder, with substantial independent clue material (Battle of Gaixia, Feast at Hong Gate, Chu-Han Contention); strong candidate for his own topic
Wang Mang — recurring figure with independent bonus-part answerline presence (Xin Dynasty, Red Eyebrows, well-field system); candidate for his own topic
Emperor Wu (Wudi) — recurring figure with independent presence (heavenly horses, salt/iron monopolies, Xiongnu wars); candidate for his own topic
Sima Qian — recurring figure with independent presence (castration, Records of the Grand Historian); category filter to History or Literature as needed
Yellow Turban Rebellion — recurring event with independent bonus-part answerline presence; candidate for its own topic
Trung Sisters' Rebellion — recurring event with independent bonus-part answerline presence; candidate for its own topic

heqin — has its own independent answerline (see the 2023 'Questions Based on Wikipedia Links Ophir Sent Me' tossup) distinct from this dynasty page; excluded here except where directly clued as identifying the Han dynasty
Xiongnu — related but distinct topic; excluded here except where it directly overlaps with Han frontier wars
Empress Lu (Lu Zhi) — recurring figure with some independent bonus-part presence; watch for overlap if she gets her own topic
Three Kingdoms period — related but distinct topic beginning at this dynasty's collapse; excluded here except at the transition
